title: First Noise
subtitle: patterns · mini-notation · the smallest meaningful sounds in history

pieces.title: Chest Opening Jingle
pieces.concept: 4 notes ascending. Every human alive recognizes it. The smallest meaningful musical gesture. This is what sequencing sounds like.

pieces.title: Coin Sound
pieces.concept: Two notes. A reward signal burned into collective memory. Pitch, duration, and why sound design is composition.

repertoire.why: Before you can write a symphony you need to write 4 notes that mean something. Kondo did it in 1986 and 40 years later you still hear it in your sleep.

dirdug.composition: Sparse koto pattern. 3-4 notes placed in silence. The first gesture of The North Atlantic.
dirdug.technique: Pattern placement. Where you put sounds matters more than which sounds you pick.

grymoire.essay: A Song, an Heir, and the Ruler of All
grymoire.connection: Rome is where the journey starts. The first sound of the Ðirðug score mirrors the first essay: a single gesture that sets everything in motion.

blocks.content: Press play. Don't read anything else yet.

You just played the Zelda chest opening jingle. Four notes. Ascending. Koji Kondo wrote this in 1986. You heard it and your brain went I found something valuable. That's what a pattern does.

Now this one:

Two notes. The Mario coin. You've heard this ten thousand times. It means you got something. Two notes, and they carry meaning.

That's composition. Sounds placed in time that make a human feel something.

blocks.label: What just happened
blocks.content: note("c4 e4 g4 c5") is a pattern. It tells Strudel which notes to play and in what order. The notes cycle through one per beat. That's all a pattern is: a sequence of events in time.
Strudel's mini-notation is how you write patterns. Let's take the Zelda jingle apart.
One note. Repeating. That's a pattern with one event. Now add the second:
Two notes. They split the cycle evenly. Each gets half the time. Add the third and fourth:

blocks.label: Mini-notation
blocks.content: Spaces separate events. More events = each one gets less time. "c4 e4 g4 c5" means four notes, evenly spaced across one cycle. This is mini-notation. Strudel's way of writing rhythmic sequences in a single string.

blocks.label: Try this
blocks.content: Change c5 to e5. What happens to the feeling? Now try g3 instead. The pattern is the same (four notes, evenly spaced) but the pitch changes everything.
Now let's get percussive:
s() instead of note(). This plays samples instead of synth notes. bd is a kick drum. hh is a hi-hat. sd is a snare. Same pattern logic: four events, evenly spaced.

blocks.label: Try this
blocks.content: Swap sd for cp (clap). Try "bd bd sd bd". Try "bd hh sd hh bd hh sd oh". Eight events now. Double-time. The pattern language is the same. Only the density changes.
One pattern is a melody. Two patterns playing at the same time is a texture. Strudel uses stack() to play multiple patterns simultaneously.

blocks.code:
stack(
  note("c4 e4 g4 c5").s("triangle").gain(0.5),
  s("bd hh sd hh")
)

blocks.content: The Zelda jingle over a basic beat. Two layers. This is how all music works. Independent voices happening at the same time. A melody doesn't exist alone. It exists against something.

blocks.label: Repertoire note: Kondo's method
blocks.content: Koji Kondo composed on the NES. 5 audio channels: 2 pulse waves, 1 triangle, 1 noise, 1 sample. Every sound in every Nintendo game came from those 5 voices. You're working with the same idea. stack() lets you layer voices, but each one has to earn its place.

blocks.label: Try this
blocks.content: Add a third layer: note("c3").s("sawtooth").lpf(400). A low filtered bass note. Feel how it changes the weight of everything above it.

Every lesson in this course builds a piece of music for Ðirðug: The Long Road North. A documentary expedition. Rome to Atlanta. Germanic migrations, traced in reverse.

Your first composition uses a koto, a 13-string Japanese instrument plucked with fingerpicks. It sounds like a harp with edges. .sound("gm_koto") loads it from Strudel's General MIDI bank. Three or four notes placed in silence. The beginning of The North Atlantic. The crossing between Norway and Iceland, the passage Guðríður made a thousand years ago in an open ship.

Sparse. Mostly silence. The ~ is a rest, a deliberate gap. The delay and room let each note ring out and decay into the space around it. This is the sound of open water.

This is the seed. Over the next 7 lessons, you'll add a pulse, a drone, a second voice, and effects. By Lesson 7, you'll have a finished 60-90 second piece.

blocks.label: Your turn
blocks.content: Change the notes. Keep the silences. The ~ rests are doing as much work as the notes. Try "a3 ~ ~ ~ d4 ~ ~ e4". Try your own. Find 3-4 notes that feel like grey water and distance.
Put it all together. A beat, a melody, and your Ðirðug sketch layered on top. This is yours. Change anything. Break it. Fix it. Share it.

blocks.label: Before you go
items: Replace the Zelda melody with the Mario coin (note("b4 e5")). How does 2 notes vs 4 change the feel?
Write a 6-note pattern of your own. No theory. Just notes that sound good to you.
Remove the beat entirely. Just the Ðirðug sketch alone. Does it stand on its own?
Share your capstone. Hit the share button and post it.
